title: Constants & Macros 🔒
description: Lock down variables so they can't be changed.

slides.title: Read-Only Data 🔒
slides.content:
Sometimes you want a variable's value to stay exactly the same throughout your entire program (like the value of Pi, or the speed of light).

If you lock it down, you prevent accidental bugs where you overwrite data you shouldn't.

slides.title: The const Keyword
slides.content: Adding the `const` keyword before a variable declaration makes it read-only. If you try to change it later, the compiler will yell at you in red ink!

quizData.question: What happens if you attempt to assign a new value to a `const` variable?

options.text: The program just ignores the assignment.
The compiler throws an error and refuses to compile.

quizData.explanation: const means 'read-only'. The compiler strictly enforces this to prevent bugs.

slides.title: Macros (#define) 🤯
slides.content:
C has an older, powerful alternative to `const` called Macros.

You use `#define` at the very top of your file. When the code compiles, the preprocessor literally finds and replaces the macro name with the value everywhere in your code.

quizData.question: In a `#define` macro, what symbol do you put at the end of the line?
quizData.explanation: Macros are pre-processor directives, not C statements. Therefore they do NOT use a semicolon at the end.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## Syntax Differences

Notice that `#define` does NOT have an `=` sign, and it does NOT end with a semicolon `;`.

* `const float PI = 3.14;`
* `#define PI 3.14`
